hero.tagline: Autonomous mobile robots - the warehouse and hospital workhorse
hero.intro: An AMR (Autonomous Mobile Robot) is a navigation stack on wheels - it senses obstacles, plans around them, talks to a fleet manager, charges itself, and integrates with the warehouse software that tells it what to move where. AMRs are the most-deployed mobile robot in the world today, in fulfillment centers, hospitals, factories, and last-mile delivery. This branch covers the full stack: drive systems, indoor localization, fleet protocols (VDA 5050), charging and docking, safety certification (ANSI R15.08), and how it all plugs into a WMS. If your job involves a robot that has to live in a real building, read this.

sections.heading: AMR vs AGV vs autonomous vehicle
sections.body: These terms get confused constantly. AGVs (Automated Guided Vehicles) follow fixed guidance - magnetic tape, painted lines, embedded wires, or QR codes on the floor. They can't deviate. AMRs (Autonomous Mobile Robots) build a map and localize on it; they can re-route around new obstacles, take alternate paths, and operate in shared human spaces. Autonomous vehicles (cars, trucks, drones) are the same idea but at higher speeds and in unstructured outdoor environments - the algorithms overlap heavily but the certification, sensors, and dynamics differ.
bullets: AGV - fixed path, lower cost, decades-old, brittle to layout changes
AMR - free navigation, more sensors + compute, handles dynamic obstacles, expensive per unit
Self-driving - same SLAM/perception stack, much higher speeds, road-specific perception (lane lines, traffic lights), regulated differently
Hybrid AGV - modern AGVs increasingly add dynamic obstacle avoidance, blurring the line
Picking the right one: AGVs win on cost when paths are fixed; AMRs win when layouts change weekly

sections.heading: Drive systems - diff drive, mecanum, ackermann, swerve
sections.body: An AMR's drivetrain is the first design decision and it constrains everything downstream. Differential drive (TurtleBot, MiR) is cheap, robust, and turns in place - but can't sidestep. Mecanum (KUKA KMR, Fetch) gives full holonomic motion at the cost of efficiency and slip. Ackermann (cars) needs forward motion to turn - wrong choice for tight warehouses. Swerve drive (FRC robots, some industrial AMRs) gives independently steered wheels - best of all worlds but mechanically complex.
bullets: Differential drive - 2 driven + 1+ casters; vx + ωz only, can rotate in place
Mecanum - 4 wheels with 45° rollers; full vx/vy/ωz; ~30% efficiency loss to roller slip
Ackermann - 2 driven + 2 steered; minimum turning radius; outdoor / fast
Swerve - 4 independently driven + steered modules; full holonomic, very mechanically complex
Tracked - 2 tracks; high traction off-road; high wear, scrubs floor, banned indoors

codeBlocks.title: Mecanum kinematics - body twist (vx, vy, ωz) → 4 wheel speeds
codeBlocks.code:
import numpy as np

def mecanum_ik(vx, vy, wz, lx, ly, r):
    # lx, ly = half-distances between wheels in x and y
    # r = wheel radius
    L = lx + ly
    return (1 / r) * np.array([
        vx - vy - L * wz,   # front-left
        vx + vy + L * wz,   # front-right
        vx + vy - L * wz,   # rear-left
        vx - vy + L * wz    # rear-right
    ])

codeBlocks.title: Ackermann kinematics - vx + steering angle → ωz
codeBlocks.code:
import math

def ackermann_omega(vx, steer_angle, wheel_base):
    # steer_angle is the front virtual-wheel angle (bicycle model)
    return vx * math.tan(steer_angle) / wheel_base

# Minimum turning radius at max steer
min_r = wheel_base / math.tan(max_steer_rad)

sections.heading: Indoor localization - beyond AMCL
sections.body: AMCL on a 2D LiDAR works in most warehouses but fails in featureless spaces (long corridors, glass walls, mostly-empty floors). Production AMRs layer on additional cues - UWB beacons for absolute position, fiducial markers for known landmarks, ceiling-mounted cameras with QR codes, Wi-Fi / BLE fingerprinting as a coarse fallback, and wheel odometry as the always-on backbone. The fleet manager often blends all of these via robot_localization or a custom EKF.
bullets: Wheel odometry - always available, drifts ~5% per metre (worse on slick floors)
2D LiDAR + AMCL - dominant indoor solution, breaks in symmetric spaces
UWB anchors (Decawave, Nooploop) - ~10 cm accuracy, infrastructure cost
Fiducial markers (AprilTag, ArUco) - cheap absolute fixes at chokepoints
Floor QR codes (Kiva-style) - when you control the floor and want determinism
VIO + IMU - backup when LiDAR is occluded by people

codeBlocks.title: robot_localization - fuse wheel odom + IMU + AMCL pose
codeBlocks.code:
ekf_filter_node:
  ros__parameters:
    frequency: 30.0
    two_d_mode: true
    publish_tf: true

    odom0: /wheel/odom
    odom0_config: [false, false, false,
                   false, false, false,
                   true,  false, false,
                   false, false, true,
                   false, false, false]

    imu0: /imu/data
    imu0_config: [false, false, false,
                  false, false, true,
                  false, false, false,
                  false, false, true,
                  false, false, false]

    pose0: /amcl_pose
    pose0_config: [true,  true,  false,
                   false, false, true,
                   false, false, false,
                   false, false, false,
                   false, false, false]
    pose0_differential: false

sections.heading: Fleet architecture - Open-RMF and friends
sections.body: Once you have more than two robots, you need a fleet manager - software that knows where each robot is, what task each is doing, how to dispatch the next pickup, and how to keep robots from blocking each other at intersections. Open-RMF (Robotics Middleware Framework) is the open-source standard, originally from the Singapore healthcare deployment. Commercial alternatives: Vecna, MiR Fleet, OTTO Fleet, AutoStore (different category). Most fleet managers expose REST + MQTT APIs for integration.
bullets: Fleet manager - central brain, talks to N robots over the network
Traffic supervision - graph of waypoints + lanes; locks segments to prevent collisions
Task dispatcher - receives orders (from WMS / MES), assigns to nearest available robot
Building integration - Open-RMF talks to elevators, doors, charging stations as 'asset adapters'
Multi-vendor - VDA 5050 lets you mix robots from different vendors under one fleet manager

codeBlocks.title: Stand up Open-RMF in Docker
codeBlocks.code:
docker run --rm -it --network host \
    -v $HOME/{{ws}}/maps:/maps \
    ghcr.io/open-rmf/rmf:latest \
    ros2 launch rmf_demos office.launch.xml

sections.heading: VDA 5050 - vendor-neutral fleet protocol
sections.body: VDA 5050 is the German Automotive Industry Association's open standard for AMR ↔ fleet manager communication. It uses MQTT to exchange JSON messages - the fleet sends an `order` (graph of waypoints + actions), the robot publishes `state` (pose, battery, errors) and `visualization` (path), and either side can request `instantActions`. It's how you mix robots from MiR, Fetch, OTTO, and your own custom builds under a single fleet manager.
bullets: Topics: orders, state, visualization, connection, instantActions, factsheet
Transport: MQTT 3.1.1 / 5.0 - typically port 1883 inside the warehouse network
Versioned: as of 2025, v2.0 is widely deployed; v2.1 adds a few action types
Implementations exist in Python, C++, .NET - most vendors ship one of these
Adoption is uneven - read each vendor's compliance matrix before promising interop

codeBlocks.title: VDA 5050 order - drive A → B, then pick
Subscribe to a robot's state
codeBlocks.code:
import json, paho.mqtt.client as mqtt

def on_message(client, _, msg):
    state = json.loads(msg.payload)
    print(f"battery: {state['batteryState']['batteryCharge']:.0%}, "
          f"pose: ({state['agvPosition']['x']:.2f}, {state['agvPosition']['y']:.2f})")

c = mqtt.Client('fleet-manager')
c.connect('broker.local', 1883)
c.subscribe('uagv/v2/AcmeBots/AMR-001/state')
c.on_message = on_message
c.loop_forever()

sections.heading: Charging & docking
sections.body: Battery life kills more AMR pilots than navigation does. The robot needs to know when it's getting low, schedule a return to the dock, drive precisely onto the contacts, charge to a strategic state-of-charge, and undock without bumping anything. Most fleets keep robots in the 20-80% SOC band to extend pack life - that means more charge cycles, smaller per-cycle energy.
bullets: Contact charging - copper bus + spring-loaded contact pads; cheap, reliable, requires precise alignment
Inductive / wireless - no contact wear; lower efficiency (75-85%); higher cost
Hot-swap battery - manual but eliminates downtime; requires staff
Opportunity charging - short top-ups during natural wait times (e.g., conveyor delays)
Dock detection: AprilTag on dock + lidar reflectors; servo into pose with vision-based PID
Fleet-level charge scheduling - keep N% of fleet charging at any time, rotate

codeBlocks.title: Servo onto a dock using AprilTag pose feedback
codeBlocks.code:
from rclpy.node import Node
from apriltag_msgs.msg import AprilTagDetectionArray
from geometry_msgs.msg import Twist

class DockServo(Node):
    def __init__(self):
        super().__init__('dock_servo')
        self.create_subscription(AprilTagDetectionArray,
            '/dock/tags', self.cb, 10)
        self.cmd = self.create_publisher(Twist, '/cmd_vel', 10)

    def cb(self, msg):
        if not msg.detections: return self.cmd.publish(Twist())
        d = msg.detections[0]
        # Tag pose in camera frame: position.x = lateral, position.z = forward distance
        lateral = d.pose.pose.pose.position.x
        forward = d.pose.pose.pose.position.z - 0.05  # 5 cm offset
        twist = Twist()
        twist.linear.x  = max(min(0.5 * forward, 0.05), -0.05)
        twist.angular.z = max(min(-1.5 * lateral, 0.3), -0.3)
        self.cmd.publish(twist)

sections.heading: Safety certification - ANSI R15.08 & ISO 13482
sections.body: An AMR moving through a warehouse with humans is a regulated machine. ANSI R15.08 (US) and ISO 3691-4 (Europe - lift trucks, but applied to AMRs) define the safety functions: protective stop, emergency stop, safety-rated speed and force limits, and the safety-rated PLd / SIL2 control architecture that enforces them. The safety stack is separate from the autonomy stack - a dedicated safety PLC reads safety-rated lidar scans (SICK microScan3, Hokuyo UAM) and cuts power to the motors if anything enters the protected field at speed.
bullets: Two parallel control stacks: 'autonomy' (best-effort) and 'safety' (must-not-fail)
Safety-rated lidar - SICK microScan3 / nanoScan3, Hokuyo UAM; PLd, SIL2 certified
Safety PLC - Pilz, SICK Flexi Soft, Beckhoff TwinSAFE; runs the safety logic deterministically
Speed-zone management - slower in human zones, faster in caged areas
ANSI R15.08-1 (machine), R15.08-2 (system), R15.08-3 (user) - three-part US standard
Risk assessment per layout - required deliverable before deployment

sections.heading: WMS / MES / ERP integration
sections.body: Robots are useless if they don't know what to move. The Warehouse Management System (WMS - Manhattan, Blue Yonder, Körber, SAP EWM) holds inventory and orders. The fleet manager subscribes to pick / putaway tasks from the WMS and dispatches them to robots. Most integrations are REST-over-HTTPS or AMQP/MQTT; SAP shops often use IDoc or SAP MM. Standardization is poor - every WMS exposes its own task contract.
bullets: WMS - what to move, from where, to where, by when (inventory truth)
MES - manufacturing execution; AMRs feed parts to assembly lines
Fleet manager - translates WMS tasks → robot motion + actions
Common integration patterns: REST polling, MQTT pub/sub, AMQP queues
Latency budget: typical pick task creation → first robot motion < 5 s
Idempotency + retries - networks fail; tasks must not double-execute

codeBlocks.title: Pull pending tasks from a WMS REST API
codeBlocks.code:
import httpx, asyncio

async def pull_tasks(wms_url, token):
    async with httpx.AsyncClient(timeout=10) as c:
        while True:
            r = await c.get(
                f'{wms_url}/api/v1/tasks?status=READY',
                headers={'Authorization': f'Bearer {token}'})
            r.raise_for_status()
            for task in r.json()['tasks']:
                await dispatch_to_fleet(task)
            await asyncio.sleep(2)

sections.heading: Traffic management & conflict resolution
sections.body: Two AMRs heading toward the same intersection is a coordination problem, not a navigation problem - each robot's local planner alone can't solve it. Open-RMF uses a graph-based traffic supervisor: paths are sequences of lane segments, and the supervisor locks segments before robots traverse them. Conflicts are resolved by negotiation (the lower-priority robot reroutes or waits). Other approaches: token-based (one token per intersection), reservation tables (TEA*), and CBS-style multi-agent path finding for tighter optimization.
bullets: Lane graph - explicit map of traversable segments + intersections
Reservation table - robots claim (segment, time) tuples; conflicts denied
Priority schemes: FIFO, payload-weighted, deadline-driven
Deadlock prevention: detect cycles in wait-for graph; force one robot to back off
MAPF (CBS, ECBS, PBS) - optimal multi-robot pathfinding, expensive at scale
Practical fallback: if planner can't resolve, robots stop and ask human operator

sections.heading: Legged AMRs - gaits and quadrupeds
sections.body: Wheeled AMRs dominate flat warehouse floors, but legged platforms (Boston Dynamics Spot, Unitree Go2/H1, Anymal, Ghost Robotics) are taking over inspection routes that include stairs, gravel, slopes, and unstructured outdoor terrain. The hard parts: planning footsteps, maintaining balance under disturbance, and choosing which gait (walk, trot, gallop) to use based on speed and stability requirements. The widget below shows the canonical quadruped gait patterns - try the buttons to see how Spot, a horse, and a cheetah differ.
bullets: Walk - three legs always grounded, max stability, slow
Trot - diagonal pairs swing together, dynamic but still recoverable, default for Spot/Anymal
Pace / bound / gallop - increasingly aerial, faster, less stable
Quasi-static (walk) vs dynamic (trot+) - different control architectures
MPC for dynamic gaits - re-plan footstep + body trajectory at 100+ Hz
RL policies (Anymal, Cassie) increasingly replace hand-engineered controllers

codeBlocks.title: Bring up Unitree Go2 in simulation (Isaac Lab)

sections.heading: Building an AMR - TurtleBot 4 to custom
sections.body: Working backwards from the easiest path: TurtleBot 4 is a complete AMR for prototyping. Clearpath Husky / Dingo are the next step up - bigger battery, better motors, IP-rated chassis. Beyond that, you're designing your own - picking a motor controller (RoboteQ, ODrive, custom), a safety lidar, a charging system, building the chassis, and integrating it all. The software stack stays largely the same: ROS 2 + Nav2 + your fleet manager.

codeBlocks.title: Bring up a Clearpath Husky in simulation
codeBlocks.code:
sudo apt install -y ros-jazzy-husky-desktop ros-jazzy-husky-simulator
ros2 launch husky_gazebo husky_playpen.launch.py
# In another terminal:
ros2 launch husky_navigation amcl_demo.launch.py

codeBlocks.title: Custom URDF skeleton for a small AMR
codeBlocks.code:
<?xml version="1.0"?>
<robot name="amr_lite">
  <link name="base_link">
    <visual>
      <geometry><box size="0.6 0.5 0.25"/></geometry>
    </visual>
    <inertial>
      <mass value="30.0"/>
      <inertia ixx="1.2" iyy="1.5" izz="2.4"
               ixy="0" ixz="0" iyz="0"/>
    </inertial>
  </link>
  <!-- 2 driven wheels, 2 casters, lidar mount, top plate ... -->
</robot>
